State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance Administration • • • Executive Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444

May 22, 1978

Timothy Kip Finn
Munden and Finn, Ltd.
Certified Public Accountants
14 Commercial Blvd., Suite 105
Novato, CA 94947

Dear Mr. Finn:

     Thank you for your recent letter in which you ask
whether there are any laws or regulations on the practice
of government officials using their names, rather than
the name of the office, when conducting governmental business.
You believe this practice to be unfair.

     The Political Reform Act regulates the use of govern-
ment paid mailings by state officials. Government Code
Section 89001* prohibits legislative newsletters or other
mass mailings from being sent at state expense after the
state official has filed the declaration of candidacy.
Thus, for a period of about eight months the official can-
not send such material.

     Elections Code Section 11800 applies a similar rule
to local officials. It says that no mass mailing by local
officials can be sent after the official files a declaration
of candidacy. However, Elections Code Section 11801 exempts
official mailings required by law.

     Although this provision in the Elections Code is very
similar to the Political Reform Act section, the Commission
is not empowered to interpret it nor bring enforcement
actions. Our mandate is limited to the Government Code,
beginning with Section 81000, et seq.

* All statutory references are to the Government Code unless
  otherwise noted.

Timothy Kip Furn
Page Two                                 May 22, 1978


     In interpreting Section 89001, the Commission has
recognized that not all mailings by government officials
can be prohibited.  The Commission by regulation (18901),
a copy of which is enclosed, has said that the following
mailings, among others, are permissible even though the
official's name is on the letter and envelope:

     a.  Letters in response to inquiries,

     b.  Press releases sent to the media,

     c.  Mailings required by statute

     The Commission recently issued an opinion to Secretary
of State March Fong Eu, a copy of which is enclosed, which
provides a little further guidance in this area.

     As to the specific enclosures you sent to us, I am not
sure that it is a great advantage for the tax collector to
identify himself, especially in Marin County.  We are all
aware of the taxpayers opposition to government bureaucracy,
particularly the bureaucracy that is involved with taxes.
It can be argued that there is merit to require each
government office to identify the persons who are responsible
for its decisions.

     I hope that this letter and the information I have
enclosed will be of assistance to you.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Robert M. Stern
                                        General Counsel

RMS:nc

Enclosures

MUNDEN & FINN, LTD.
CERTIFIED PUBLIC ACCOUNTANTS
14 COMMERCIAL BOULEVARD, SUITE 105
NOVATO, CALIF. 94947

415-883-7779

April 24, 1978

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento CA 95814

Honorable Commissioners:

Many elected officials enjoy a large unfair advantage
because they do government business in their own personal
names. For example, a Tax Collector may require tax pay-
ments made to his or her own name; or assessments and other
notices may be sent in the individual name of the elected
officials, and so on. Examples are enclosed. I feel that
such business could be done just as well in the title of
the office, as in the name of the individual office-holder.

This practice creates tremendous name-recognition
entirely at public expense, and is a strong disadvantage to
any would-be opponents. It also creates a powerful base for
seeking higher office. In my own case, I am running for
Treasurer-Tax Collector of Marin County partly because the
incumbent is not running for re-election. I also feel that
this incumbent advantage contributes to government inefi-
ciency and stagnation.

Is this practice contrary to any laws or regulations?
If not, are there any plans to seek remedial legislation?

Very truly yours,

Timothy Kip Finn, C.P.A.

Kronos
Quartet
to play
April 9

The Kronos Quartet will
perform in concert this
Sunday, April 9, at 3:30
p.m. at the Mill Valley
Golf Clubhouse, part of
the Mill Valley Chamber
Music Society's 1978
Spring Concert Series.
The quartet, which
includes violinists David
Harrington and John Sherba,
violist Hank Dutt and
cellist Walter Gray, will
play works by Mozart,
Bartok and Lucas Foss.
Admission is $3.50,
students $2. For information, phone 388-
0721.

OF YOUR COUNTY RECORDER,
NOEL GIACOMINI

Sent with all deeds

STANLEY J. FONTEZ
Marin County Tax Collector

Inasmuch as property taxes should always be considered in the transfer of real property, a new owner should be keenly aware of the taxes to which the property may be subject at the date of the purchase. We suggest that you read your title report and escrow papers carefully to ascertain which taxes were paid in closing, and which taxes you will be called upon to pay

Should you have purchased or sold a portion of an existing assessment, this presents a special problem. Be sure to contact the Tax Collector's office and apply for separate billing. In order to obtain separate billing, the book and page number of recorded deed must be given to the Tax Collector.

III. SOLD FOR DELINQUENT TAXES

IF THE WORDS "SOLD FOR DELINQUENT TAXES" APPEAR ON YOUR TAX BILL, IT IS AN INDICATION THAT PRIOR YEARS TAXES ARE STILL UNPAID AND ARE ACCRUING PENALTIES. FOR SPECIFIC INFORMATION AS TO THE AMOUNT DUE OR METHOD OF PAYMENT, DO NOT HESITATE TO CONTACT THE TAX COLLECTOR'S OFFICE BY PHONE OR LETTER.

ALL QUESTIONS PERTAINING TO ASSESSED VALUES, HOMEOWNER'S EXEMPTION ON ALL PROPERTY SHOULD BE REFERRED TO THE COUNTY ASSESSOR'S OFFICE, AS THAT IS THE OFFICE RESPONSIBLE FOR THIS FUNCTION.

IN THE EVENT THAT YOU ARE IN DOUBT ABOUT THE STATUS OF YOUR TAXES, PLEASE CONTACT THE OFFICE OR STANLEY J. FONTEZ, MARIN COUNTY TAX COLLECTOR, CIVIC CENTER, SAN RAFAEL, TELEPHONE 479-1100.
